speaker: interviewer
text: A new addition has just been made. Would you please introduce yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sylvia [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how are you related?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is a survivor of the Holocaust. And I was born in 1946 in a--

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: DP camp, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah [? Kindleman. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chana's sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chana's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could we continue in terms of the children having anything to say? Lucy, is there anything that you would like to say to your parents and how it affected you growing up with a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm just happy that he-- he survived and was able to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bonnie, is there anything that you would like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it sort of effects me more now than it did when I was a child. Like, it was normal to me, you know? In a way. And now, when I think about it, I think it's amazing what they did and how they survived. I couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You would.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to hear from [? Sima ?] as having been the youngest to survive the cave experience. Is there anything that you would like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? SIMA BLITZER: ?] Well, I'm [? Sima ?] Blitzer. I'm the younger daughter of Henia and Fischel Dodek. My mother was the oldest of the six Stermer siblings. I don't remember that much. I'm sure my uncles remember far more than I do. But the values that I took out of this war was that my grandmother taught me wisdom and courage. It wouldn't be for my grandmother's wisdom, we would have ended up in concentration camps. It wouldn't be for her courage that she went out to speak for all of us. I'm sure my uncle told you about the first grotto, that we were discovered. She went out, and she spoke. And she could have easily been killed and shot immediately. But she decided that it was her role to give her family a chance to hide. My mother also taught me a lot. My mother was called in my grandmother's book, 'the rose of the family.' She was the eldest daughter. She was kind. She was very gentle. And she taught me that you have to share in the worst of times. She took in a woman when they were driving the Jews out of the Carpathians. She took a woman in with a broken arm, took her into her house, and nursed her back to health and kept her for two years. And she fed her. And those were very difficult times to feed anyone, because as I'm sure my uncle told you, a piece of bread meant surviving another day. My sister made a film. My sister is [? Sonia Hauchman. ?] She lives in New York. And she herself made a film of our experiences. I can say that this is a very courageous family. My uncle [INAUDIBLE] played a major, as did my other aunts and uncles. I'd like to speak about my Aunt [? Hanna ?] who unfortunately is not here. My Aunt [? Hanna ?] was beautiful woman. And her husband was in Siberia at the time. [CRYING] Sorry, I shouldn't be so emotional. It's all right. [? SIMA BLITZER: ?] And after the war, when my father Fischel Dodek was killed, she and her husband, [? Josef ?] Richter, really acted as surrogate parents to me. The other person I wish to speak about is my father Fischel Dodek, may he rest in peace. And his memory's very loving to me. In the first action, he lost his entire family, my bubbe [? Brancha, ?] [INAUDIBLE], or his brothers and sisters except for one who survived miraculously. Unfortunately, my father was killed by the Ukrainians immediately after the war. He survived the entire war, fought very bravely, and took care of the entire family-- my mother, my sister Sonia, myself, and of two cousins. He was very brave. I don't know why this happened to him. There are no answers, of course. Because he was brave. [INAUDIBLE]. [? SIMA BLITZER: ?] I have nothing but wonderful things to say about my aunts and my uncles and about my bubbe Esther. She lived with me, or I lived with her all my life. And she really set the principles of values, of taking care of the family, for all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing that with us, [? Sima. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to mention that I have a husband, David Blitzer. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] [? SIMA BLITZER: ?] And he's also a survivor. And I have two children. One is-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] [? Gita ?] Blitzer, and she's married to Bobby [? Shawl ?] of Toronto. They have three lovely children, Daniel [? Yahuda ?] who's nine, [? Henya Eliana Shawl, ?] who's named after my mother. She is six. And a little boy Jonathan. I have a daughter Bonnie who lives in Boston. She's married to [? Eli Hugh Stone. ?] They have two beautiful children, Juliana Stone and [? Yahuda ?] Stone, who's named after my father, as is Daniel [? Yahuda ?] Stone, one of the names after my father. And he is expecting another baby. I-- I want to say that if we have here a second generation and a third generation, this is to show Hitler that he didn't win, that we're stronger, and we're continuing. We fight to survive. [? SIMA BLITZER: ?] We truly fight to survive. Amen.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? SIMA BLITZER: ?] Thank you. Thank you. Sam, is there anything that you would like to say? SAM (BROTHER): Very emotional. I can understand why. [? SIMA BLITZER: ?] As we all are.

speaker: interviewee
text: SAM (BROTHER): Before I start, I-- Before he starts, he cries. SAM (BROTHER): All I can say is that, you know, like my brother told you how before, how we together and how my mother saved me when they took us out, the Germans. I-- I told-- SAM (BROTHER): When I-- when I heard the Germans, I grabbed an ax and said, let's kill one. But I was very young. And-- and I said-- I thought nobody would come here with me, so I threw it away. And I grabbed the pillow over my head and says, I don't want to see when they shoot me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- then all of a sudden, I heard my mother talk. I said, hey, there's still a chance. So the German-- so I-- I got off from the bed. And You were in-- you know, we were not dressed. It's just we-- you know, my mother spoke to him and all that. And I-- I lost my fear. And she-- and he's just started right [INAUDIBLE]. He says, get dressed, fast. I wasn't-- I didn't fear at all. So I just-- slowly I put on my sweater, my jacket, my vest, my pants, my shoes, my-- my boots. And I put the piece of cloth around them. And it was-- and I looked just in that thing. They took me out. So they gave everybody a little lamp. So my mother hit me over the hand right away, [INAUDIBLE] that. So right away, gave me the idea that--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. SAM (BROTHER): I'll have to find a way to get away. Because I knew the-- the cave-- I was there six months-- every hole. You know, when you're 16 and 17, in every hole, I knew without-- without a light, I could go any place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they took us out, there was one German in the back. And I was under my mother. I was a little young kid. And when they took us out, there were two guys. You know, Obersturmfuhrer, you know? And-- and four of them was standing with machine guns with the lights, and-- and they-- they lined us up, including [? Sima, ?] my sister, my-- my cousin. You know, eight people of us. And there's a doctor. And as soon as they lined us up outside the-- our headquarters. But it was far to go outside. And I was and my mother was standing behind-- you know, with the guard, the German, the back of me. They all got together, so probably decide, what shall we do? Should we kill him here or kill-- kill him outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- so they got together, and they started talking. As soon as they got together, my-- my mother hit me. Move! SAM (BROTHER): And I took off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks you. You ran to survival. I hope one day we can hear your story on a testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hey has a big story. SAM (BROTHER): I have a big-- Yes, Nissen, did you want to say something else? Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: After liberation, I promised myself that-- [INAUDIBLE]. I [INAUDIBLE]. Then they say, children, mission accomplished. Let's move on.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] He-- he-- he got-- he got-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: May I ask, in closing, from the youngest member of this gathering--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewer
text: Erin. Do you have anything that you would like to say for your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm really amazed and impressed what he and his brothers and sisters did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you must be very proud to be part of this family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. SAM (BROTHER): I just want to mention that I have-- that's my wife. And I have two children. I have a daughter, 43, and a son 39.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're names.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I have six grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mazel tov. SAM (BROTHER): Their names--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children's--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] SAM (BROTHER): Myla, Irvin, that's my son and my-- and they had-- my-- have-- I have-- my daughter has three children, Benjamin--

speaker: interviewer
text: Steven. Steven. SAM (BROTHER): And her husband, Steven, naturally. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why your wife-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] SAM (BROTHER): Russell, and-- and-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. SAM (BROTHER): And-- what's [INAUDIBLE]? Marla. SAM (BROTHER): Oh, the little-- the little girls. And [? Katherine. ?] The two boys-- the three boys, and I have the three--

speaker: interviewee
text: I told us everything-- SAM (BROTHER): And [INAUDIBLE] three brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much for all being here with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And many more stories are going to come from this.
